nodes.label: Abolhassan Banisadr
nodes.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)

data.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)
data.bio: Abolhassan Banisadr was the first president of the Islamic Republic, elected in January 1980 with 76% of the vote. An economist and political thinker who had been close to Khomeini in exile, he quickly came into conflict with the clerical establishment and the Islamic Republican Party. He was impeached by parliament in June 1981 and fled to France, where he lived in exile until his death in 2021.

nodes.label: Ahmad Jannati
nodes.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts

data.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts
data.bio: Ahmad Jannati has been a member of the Guardian Council since its founding in 1980, and its secretary since 1992 — the longest-tenured figure in any constitutional body of the Islamic Republic. A founding member of the hardline Haghani Seminary in Qom alongside Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi, he is among the most extreme voices in Iranian politics, repeatedly calling for the execution of political opponents and systematically disqualifying reformist candidates. He served concurrently as Chairman of the Assembly of Experts. His continuation in office into his late nineties has been attributed to political calculation over capacity.

nodes.label: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani
nodes.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)

data.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)
data.bio: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani was one of the most powerful figures in post-revolutionary Iran. He served as Speaker of the Islamic Consultative Assembly from 1980 to 1989 and as president from 1989 to 1997. A cleric and confidant of Khomeini, he is associated with the pragmatist wing of Iranian politics and championed post-war economic reconstruction. He chaired the Expediency Discernment Council until his death in January 2017.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office
data.bio: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri served as Speaker of Parliament for two full terms, 1992 to 2000. He was the establishment-backed frontrunner in the 1997 presidential election before being unexpectedly defeated by reformist Mohammad Khatami in a landslide. He subsequently served as head of the Inspection Office of the Supreme Leader's office. He represents the traditional conservative wing of Iranian politics.

nodes.label: Ali Khamenei
nodes.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)

data.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)
data.bio: Ali Khamenei served as Iran's third president from 1981 to 1989, then succeeded Ruhollah Khomeini as Supreme Leader following Khomeini's death. As Supreme Leader for nearly four decades, he exercised ultimate authority over all branches of government, the military, and the judiciary. He oversaw Iran's nuclear programme, the crackdown on the Green Movement in 2009, and the response to the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022. He died on 28 February 2026.

nodes.label: Ali Larijani
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator
data.bio: Ali Larijani is the longest-serving speaker in the history of the Islamic Republic, holding the position from 2008 to 2020. He previously served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2005–2007) and secretary of the Supreme National Security Council. He was disqualified from the 2021 presidential election by the Guardian Council. His brother Sadegh Larijani served as head of the judiciary.

nodes.label: Alireza Arafi
nodes.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Alireza Arafi is a senior cleric and Guardian Council member appointed by Khamenei in 2019. He has served as president of the Islamic Seminaries of Iran (Hawza). Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he was appointed as the Guardian Council's representative on the three-member interim leadership council, alongside President Pezeshkian and Chief Justice Mohseni-Ejei, pursuant to Article 111 of the constitution. He has been mentioned as a potential future Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Raisi
nodes.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)

data.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)
data.bio: Ebrahim Raisi was a hardline cleric who served as head of the judiciary from 2019 before being elected president in 2021 in an election with record-low turnout. He is alleged to have been a member of the 'Death Commission' that ordered the mass execution of political prisoners in 1988. He was subject to US Treasury sanctions since 2019. His presidency was dominated by the Mahsa Amini protests of 2022. He died on 19 May 2024 when his helicopter crashed in foggy conditions near the Azerbaijan border.

nodes.label: Esmail Qaani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)
data.bio: Esmail Qaani succeeded Qasem Soleimani as Quds Force commander on the day of Soleimani's assassination, appointed immediately by Khamenei. A veteran of the Iran-Iraq War and longtime Quds Force deputy, his tenure was marked by criticism that he lacked Soleimani's strategic flair and public profile. He oversaw Quds Force operations during the Gaza conflict from 2023 and the broader escalation with Israel. Iranian sources reported he was killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.bio: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel is a philosopher and politician who served as Speaker of Parliament from 2004 to 2008, the first non-cleric to hold the role since the 1979 revolution. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran and has led the Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature since 1995. His daughter Zahra married Mojtaba Khamenei, son of the Supreme Leader, making Haddad-Adel one of the most personally connected figures to the supreme leadership. He is a senior member of the principlist Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran.

nodes.label: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei is a hardline jurist appointed Chief Justice by Khamenei in July 2021. He previously served as Minister of Intelligence under Ahmadinejad (2005–2009), as prosecutor-general, and as first deputy chief justice. He is regarded as a reliable enforcer of the regime's security priorities. Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he became one of three members of the constitutional interim leadership council alongside President Pezeshkian and Guardian Council member Arafi.

nodes.label: Hassan Rouhani
nodes.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)

data.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)
data.bio: Hassan Rouhani was elected in 2013 on a centrist platform promising to ease sanctions. He oversaw negotiations that led to the 2015 JCPOA nuclear deal, the signature achievement of his presidency. After the United States withdrew from the JCPOA in 2018, Iran's economy deteriorated sharply. Prior to the presidency he served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2003–2005) and as secretary of the Supreme National Security Council for sixteen years.

nodes.label: Hossein Salami
nodes.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)

data.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)
data.bio: Hossein Salami served as Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC from April 2019, appointed by Khamenei following the removal of Mohammad Ali Jafari. He joined the IRGC in 1980 at the outbreak of the Iran-Iraq War and rose through its Aerospace Force before appointment to the top post. He oversaw the IRGC during the Mahsa Amini crackdown of 2022, the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile and drone programmes, and the direct Iranian strikes against Israel in April 2024. He was reportedly killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad
nodes.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)

data.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)
data.bio: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad served as mayor of Tehran from 2003 before his presidential election victory in 2005. His two-term presidency was defined by confrontational nuclear rhetoric, Holocaust denial, economic mismanagement, and the disputed 2009 election that triggered the Green Movement. He significantly expanded the IRGC's economic and political power. After leaving office he fell out with Khamenei and was disqualified from subsequent presidential races.

nodes.label: Masoud Pezeshkian
nodes.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)

data.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)
data.bio: Masoud Pezeshkian, a cardiac surgeon and long-serving MP for Tabriz, won the snap presidential election of July 2024 following Raisi's death. He ran on a moderate platform and was endorsed by reformist figures including Khatami and Zarif. Ethnically Azerbaijani, he is the first non-Persian-speaking president. He previously served as Minister of Health under Khatami (2001–2005).

nodes.label: Mehdi Karroubi
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader
data.bio: Mehdi Karroubi served as Speaker of the Islamic Consultative Assembly twice. A presidential candidate in 2009, he refused to accept the disputed official results and became a co-leader of the Green Movement alongside Mir-Hossein Mousavi. He has been held under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011 without trial, one of the most prominent political detainees in the Islamic Republic.

nodes.label: Mir-Hossein Mousavi
nodes.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader

data.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader
data.bio: Mir-Hossein Mousavi served as Iran's last prime minister, managing the economy through the entire Iran-Iraq War. An architect and painter by training, he was a founding figure of the Islamic Republic before gradually moving into opposition. His disputed loss in the 2009 presidential election triggered the Green Movement. He has been under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011. In 2023 he called for a referendum to fundamentally change the constitution, a significant shift from his earlier reformist position. He is a distant relative of Ali Khamenei through a shared ancestral line from Khameneh.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Ali Rajai
nodes.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)

data.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)
data.bio: Mohammad-Ali Rajai was Iran's second president, serving for less than one month after his election on 24 July 1981. A committed revolutionary and former teacher, he had previously served as prime minister. He and his prime minister Mohammad-Javad Bahonar were assassinated in a bombing at the prime minister's office on 30 August 1981, an attack attributed to the Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK).

nodes.label: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)
data.bio: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf is a senior IRGC figure who commanded the IRGC Aerospace Force and later the national police before serving as Mayor of Tehran for twelve years. He has run for president three times without success. Elected Speaker of Parliament in 2020 after the principlist sweep of that year's elections. He was one of 24 IRGC commanders who signed a 1999 letter threatening military action against student protesters, and has admitted to personally participating in street-level violence against them.

nodes.label: Mohammad Khatami
nodes.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)

data.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)
data.bio: Mohammad Khatami was elected president in a landslide in 1997, winning around 70% of the vote on a platform of civil society, rule of law, and a Dialogue Among Civilizations. His two terms represented the peak of the reformist movement, marked by a free press and attempts to improve relations with the West. His reform agenda was consistently blocked by the Guardian Council and judiciary.

nodes.label: Qasem Soleimani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)
data.bio: Qasem Soleimani rose from a working-class background in Kerman to become the architect of Iran's network of regional proxies — Hezbollah, Shia militias in Iraq, the Houthis, and forces in Syria. As Quds Force commander from 1998 until his assassination, he was widely described as the most powerful military operative in the Middle East. He directed Iranian intervention in Syria from 2012 and was the primary coordinator of Iran's Axis of Resistance. He was killed on 3 January 2020 in a US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport, authorised by President Trump.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Khomeini
nodes.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran

data.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran
data.bio: Ruhollah Khomeini was the Shia cleric and marja who led the 1979 Iranian Revolution, overthrowing the Pahlavi monarchy and establishing the Islamic Republic. He served as Supreme Leader from 1979 until his death in June 1989. He developed the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist), which forms the constitutional basis of clerical rule in Iran.

data.description: The Expediency Discernment Council of the System (Majma'-e Tashkhis-e Maslahat-e Nezam) was established by Khomeini in 1988 to resolve legislative disputes between the Majlis and Guardian Council. It also acts as an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. Membership is appointed by the Supreme Leader and typically includes former presidents, senior clerics, and military figures, making it a powerful gathering point for Iran's political elite.

The Guardian Council (Shura-ye Negahban) is a 12-member constitutional body with three mandates: vetting legislation for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution; supervising elections; and approving or disqualifying all candidates for parliament, presidency, and Assembly of Experts. Six members are Islamic jurists appointed directly by the Supreme Leader; six are civil lawyers nominated by the Chief Justice (also appointed by the Supreme Leader) and confirmed by parliament. Members serve six-year terms in staggered cohorts.

The Quds Force (Jerusalem Force) is the IRGC branch responsible for extraterritorial operations, unconventional warfare, and support to non-state allies. It reports directly to the Supreme Leader. The Quds Force supports Hezbollah in Lebanon, Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad in Gaza, the Houthis in Yemen, and Shia militias in Iraq and Syria. It has been described by the US military as analogous to a combination of the CIA and JSOC.

The Islamic Consultative Assembly (Majles-e Shura-ye Eslami), commonly known as the Majlis or Majles, is the unicameral legislature of the Islamic Republic of Iran. It currently has 290 seats, elected for four-year terms. All candidates must be vetted and approved by the Guardian Council. The Majlis can legislate, ratify treaties, and impeach the president, but all legislation must pass Guardian Council review for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), known domestically as Sepah or Pasdaran, is a multi-service elite military force established by Khomeini in May 1979. It consists of five branches: Ground Forces, Aerospace Force, Navy, Quds Force, and Basij. Its commander-in-chief reports directly to the Supreme Leader. Beyond its military role, the IRGC controls a vast economic empire including Khatam al-Anbiya Construction, multiple banks, and extensive holdings in energy, telecommunications, and construction. Estimated at 125,000 personnel.

The Office of Prime Minister was the head of government position in the Islamic Republic from 1979 to 1989. Under the 1979 constitution, executive power was divided between the president and the prime minister. The role was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, which merged its powers into the presidency. Mir-Hossein Mousavi was the last holder of the office.

The Presidency of the Islamic Republic of Iran is the second-highest office in the political system, below the Supreme Leader. The president is elected by popular vote for four-year terms, with a two-term consecutive limit. The president chairs the cabinet and is responsible for implementing the constitution. The post of prime minister was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, merging its powers into the presidency.

The Office of the Supreme Leader (Daftar-e Maqam-e Mo'azzam-e Rahbari) is the institutional apparatus of the Supreme Leader of Iran. The Supreme Leader holds ultimate authority over all branches of government, the armed forces, the judiciary, and state media under the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih. The office encompasses numerous subsidiary bodies including the Supreme Leader's Inspection Office, his representatives in the provinces, and his representatives to the IRGC and other military branches.

data.name: Green Movement Protests
data.description: Following the disputed June 2009 presidential election in which incumbent Mahmoud Ahmadinejad was declared the winner over reformist candidates Mir-Hossein Mousavi and Mehdi Karroubi, millions of Iranians took to the streets in the largest protests since the 1979 revolution. The government response was violent; the Basij and IRGC killed dozens of protesters. The movement was suppressed by late 2009, and its leaders Mousavi and Karroubi were placed under house arrest in 2011.

data.name: Iranian Revolution
data.description: The Islamic Revolution of 1979 overthrew the Pahlavi monarchy and established the Islamic Republic of Iran. Following months of mass protests, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi fled Iran on 16 January 1979. Ayatollah Khomeini returned from exile on 1 February 1979. The revolutionary government took power on 11 February 1979, the date now commemorated as the Day of Victory of the Islamic Revolution. A constitutional referendum in March confirmed the Islamic Republic, and a new theocratic constitution was approved in December 1979.

data.name: Mahsa Amini Protests (Woman, Life, Freedom)
data.description: Following the death of 22-year-old Mahsa (Zhina) Amini in morality police custody on 16 September 2022, nationwide protests erupted under the slogan Woman, Life, Freedom (Zan, Zendegi, Azadi). The protests were the most sustained unrest in the history of the Islamic Republic, continuing for months across all provinces. Security forces killed over 500 protesters according to human rights organisations. Over 15,000 people were arrested. The protests were characterised by women publicly removing and burning their headscarves.

data.name: 1997 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 1997 Iranian presidential election resulted in a landmark victory for reformist candidate Mohammad Khatami, who won approximately 70% of the vote against establishment favourite Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri. The election shocked the Iranian political establishment and is considered the beginning of the reformist era. It demonstrated the significant gap between popular preferences and the ruling establishment's expectations.

nodes.label: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash

data.name: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash
data.description: President Ebrahim Raisi, Foreign Minister Hossein Amir-Abdollahian, and six others died when their Bell 212 helicopter crashed in a fog-covered mountainous area near Jolfa in East Azerbaijan Province on 19 May 2024. The crash triggered a constitutional provision requiring a snap presidential election within fifty days. First Vice President Mohammad Mokhber served as acting president. Masoud Pezeshkian was elected as successor on 5 July 2024.

data.name: Assassination of Qasem Soleimani
data.description: Qasem Soleimani, commander of the IRGC Quds Force, was killed in a US drone strike on his convoy outside Baghdad International Airport on 3 January 2020, authorised by President Trump. Also killed was Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, deputy commander of Iraq's Popular Mobilization Forces. Iran vowed retaliation and launched ballistic missile strikes on US bases in Iraq days later. Esmail Qaani was appointed Quds Force commander the same day.
